section.title: The Widow's Offering and the Olivet Discourse
section.sectionRationale: Jesus watches a poor widow drop two coins into the treasury and declares she has given more than all the rich, for she gave all she had to live on. When the disciples admire the temple, He foretells its total ruin and unfolds the days ahead: wars and persecutions that become occasions for witness, the encircling of Jerusalem, the times of the Gentiles, and at last the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory. So He calls His people to endure, to watch, and to lift up their heads, for their redemption draws near.
section.thesis: The Lord who honors the widow's whole-hearted gift is the coming Son of Man, and between His first and second comings His people are to endure persecution as witnesses, refuse to be deceived, and keep watch, since heaven and earth will pass away but His words never will.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the poor widow's offering; the foretold destruction of the temple and the call to endure persecution; the desolation of Jerusalem and the times of the Gentiles; and the coming of the Son of Man with the call to watch.

units.label: The poor widow's offering
units.note: she gave all she had to live on

verses.text: Then Jesus looked up and saw the rich putting their gifts into the treasury,
and He saw a poor widow put in two small copper coins.
“Truly I tell you,” He said, “this poor widow has put in more than all the others.
For they all contributed out of their surplus, but she out of her poverty has put in {{bios|all she had to live on}}.”

units.label: The temple's fall and the call to endure
units.note: by your patient endurance you will gain your souls

verses.text: As some of the disciples were remarking how the temple was adorned with beautiful stones and consecrated gifts, Jesus said,
“As for what you see here, the time will come when not one stone will be left on another; every one will be thrown down.”
“Teacher,” they asked, “when will these things happen? And what will be the sign that they are about to take place?”
Jesus answered, “See to it that you are not deceived. For many will come in My name, claiming, ‘I am He,’ and, ‘The time is near.’ Do not follow them.
When you hear of wars and rebellions, do not be alarmed. These things must happen first, but the end is not imminent.”
Then He told them, “Nation will rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom.
There will be great earthquakes, famines, and pestilences in various places, along with fearful sights and great signs from heaven.
But before all this, they will seize you and persecute you. On account of My name they will deliver you to the synagogues and prisons, and they will bring you before kings and governors.
This will be your opportunity to serve as witnesses.
So make up your mind not to worry beforehand how to defend yourselves.
For I will give you speech and wisdom that none of your adversaries will be able to resist or contradict.
You will be betrayed even by parents and brothers and relatives and friends, and some of you will be put to death.
And you will be hated by everyone because of My name.
Yet not even a hair of your head will perish.
By your {{hypomone|patient endurance}} you will gain your souls.

units.label: The desolation of Jerusalem
units.note: trodden down until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled

verses.text: But when you see Jerusalem surrounded by armies, you will know that her desolation is near.
Then let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains, let those in the city get out, and let those in the country stay out of the city.
For these are the days of vengeance, to fulfill all that is written.
How miserable those days will be for pregnant and nursing mothers! For there will be great distress upon the land and wrath against this people.
They will fall by the edge of the sword and be led captive into all the nations. And Jerusalem will be trodden down by the Gentiles, until the times of the Gentiles are fulfilled.

units.label: The coming of the Son of Man and the call to watch
units.note: lift up your heads, your redemption is drawing near

verses.text: There will be signs in the sun and moon and stars, and on the earth dismay among the nations, bewildered by the roaring of the sea and the surging of the waves.
Men will faint from fear and anxiety over what is coming upon the earth, for the powers of the heavens will be shaken.
At that time they will see the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory.
When these things begin to happen, stand up and lift up your heads, because your {{apolytrosis|redemption}} is drawing near.”
Then Jesus told them a parable: “Look at the fig tree and all the trees.
When they sprout leaves, you can see for yourselves and know that summer is near.
So also, when you see these things happening, know that the kingdom of God is near.
Truly I tell you, this generation will not pass away until all these things have happened.
Heaven and earth will pass away, but My words will never pass away.
But watch yourselves, or your hearts will be weighed down by dissipation, drunkenness, and the worries of life, and that day will spring upon you suddenly like a snare.
For it will come upon all who dwell on the face of all the earth.
So {{agrypneo|keep watch}} at all times, and pray that you may have the strength to escape all that is about to happen and to stand before the Son of Man.”
Every day Jesus taught at the temple, but every evening He went out to spend the night on the Mount of Olives.
And early in the morning all the people would come to hear Him at the temple.

bios.gloss: Life, livelihood, the means of living. The widow gave not from surplus but her whole bios, everything she had to live on. The word measures her gift not by its amount but by its cost; she held nothing back, unlike the rich who gave from their abundance. God weighs the heart behind the gift.

hypomone.gloss: Patient endurance, steadfast perseverance under pressure. In days of betrayal, hatred, and persecution, the disciples are told that by their endurance they will gain their souls. It is not passive resignation but the active, faithful holding on that marks those who belong to Christ through tribulation.

apolytrosis.gloss: Redemption, deliverance secured at a price. When the world faints in terror at the shaking of the heavens, believers are told to lift up their heads because their redemption is drawing near. The coming of the Son of Man is not dread for them but the final rescue and completion of their salvation.

agrypneo.gloss: To keep awake, to be alert and watchful. Jesus commands constant spiritual wakefulness and prayer, lest the day catch His people asleep and weighed down by indulgence and anxiety. Watchfulness is the posture of those who live ready for the return of the Son of Man.

basic.context: Jesus sees the rich dropping large gifts into the temple treasury and a poor widow adding two tiny coins, and He says she gave more than all of them, because they gave from surplus but she gave everything she had to live on. When the disciples admire the temple's grandeur, He foretells that not one stone will be left on another. They ask when, and He describes the age to come: false messiahs, wars, earthquakes, and famines, but first persecution, which will become an opportunity to witness with words He supplies. He warns of Jerusalem's coming siege and desolation, the times of the Gentiles, and then the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory. When these things begin, His people are to lift up their heads, for their redemption is near. Like leaves signaling summer, the signs show the kingdom is near. Heaven and earth will pass, but His words will not, so keep watch and pray.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the widow as the living picture of the whole-hearted giving God prizes, set against the doomed grandeur of the temple. The discourse interweaves the near destruction of Jerusalem with the far coming of the Son of Man, calling disciples not to date-set but to endure, witness, watch, and hope. The certainty rests on the permanence of Christ's words.
basic.originalLanguages: The widow gave her whole bios, her means of living. In persecution, hypomone, patient endurance, gains the soul. At the world's terror, believers lift their heads because apolytrosis, redemption, draws near. And Jesus commands His people to agrypneo, to stay awake and watchful, praying for strength to stand.
basic.typology: The doomed temple points beyond itself to Christ, the true temple, and to the new creation where God dwells with His people. The Son of Man coming in a cloud fulfills Daniel's vision of the one given everlasting dominion. The days of vengeance fulfill the prophets' warnings against a faithless city.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Lord who reads the heart of the widow's gift, the true temple whose body will be destroyed and raised, and the Son of Man of Daniel's vision who will come in a cloud with power and great glory. His words are more permanent than heaven and earth, and He is the One before whom His people will stand.
basic.god: God measures gifts by the heart and the cost, not the amount. He is sovereign over history's convulsions, using even persecution for witness, and faithful to preserve His people through tribulation. He will bring redemption at the appointed day, and His word stands forever.
basic.crossReferences: Daniel 7 gives the Son of Man coming with the clouds. Mark 13 and Matthew 24 are the parallel Olivet discourses. 2 Corinthians 8 holds up sacrificial giving out of poverty. Revelation 21 shows the new creation where no temple is needed, for God Himself is its temple.
basic.oneStory: In His last public teaching Jesus honors a widow's total gift and unveils the shape of the age between His comings: the temple will fall, His people will suffer and witness, and the Son of Man will come in glory to redeem. The story runs from the treasury to the clouds of heaven, calling His people to give, endure, watch, and hope in words that will never pass away.

context.literary: Luke frames the Olivet discourse with the widow's offering before it and Jesus' daily temple teaching after it, tying the coming judgment to the worship life of the temple. This is Luke's version of the eschatological discourse, distinct in weaving the fall of Jerusalem tightly with the return of the Son of Man. It closes the temple-teaching section and leads directly into the passion.
context.historical: The temple, rebuilt lavishly by Herod, was the pride of the nation, which makes Jesus' prophecy of its ruin shocking; it was fulfilled when Rome destroyed it in AD 70. The widow's two lepta were the smallest coins in circulation. Persecution before synagogues, kings, and governors describes what the church would soon face, recorded in Acts. The times of the Gentiles marks the long era of the gospel going to the nations.
context.canonical: The Son of Man coming in a cloud draws directly on Daniel 7, a title Jesus claims throughout the Gospels. The permanence of God's word over a passing creation echoes Isaiah 40. The call to watchfulness and readiness runs through the New Testament's teaching on the return of Christ, from the parables to the epistles and Revelation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To commend whole-hearted, sacrificial devotion in the widow, to prepare disciples for the fall of Jerusalem and the tribulations of the age by calling them to endurance, witness, and watchfulness, and to fix their hope on the certain coming of the Son of Man to redeem His people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The widow's offering is narrated but held up as an example of the giving God honors. The discourse predicts real events, near and far, and prescribes the response: do not be deceived, do not fear, endure, witness, watch, pray, and lift up your heads. The prophecy is not a timetable to calculate but a summons to faithful readiness.

debates.question: Does the discourse speak of the fall of Jerusalem or the end of the age?

views.label: Both, woven together, with a near and a far horizon
views.case: Luke clearly distinguishes the siege of Jerusalem, 'surrounded by armies', fulfilled in AD 70, from the cosmic coming of the Son of Man 'in a cloud'. The near judgment on the city becomes a pattern and pledge of the final judgment and redemption, so the two horizons are related but not identical.

views.label: Primarily the destruction of Jerusalem in that generation
views.case: The saying 'this generation will not pass away until all these things have happened' and the vivid details of the siege lead some to read most of the discourse as fulfilled in AD 70, with the coming of the Son of Man understood as His vindication and the vindication of His words in that judgment.

debates.bearing: Either way the discourse binds together a real, dated judgment and the sure hope of Christ's coming, so that His people in every generation live by endurance, watchfulness, and confidence in His unfailing word.

debates.question: Why does Jesus point to the widow rather than the large gifts?

views.label: God measures giving by cost and heart, not amount
views.case: The rich gave from surplus, losing nothing; the widow gave her whole livelihood, holding nothing back. Jesus teaches that God weighs the proportion and devotion of a gift, not its size, so the smallest offering given in total trust is the greatest in His sight.

views.label: A lament over a system that consumed the poor
views.case: Set right after the warning that the scribes devour widows' houses, some read the scene as Jesus grieving that a corrupt temple has taken even this destitute woman's last coins, exposing a religion that exploits the vulnerable it should protect.

debates.bearing: Whether primarily commendation or lament, the passage exalts whole-hearted, trusting devotion and warns against a religion of impressive surplus that costs the heart nothing.

study.originalLanguages: The widow gave her whole bios, not spare change but the very means by which she lived, so that Jesus, who weighs the cost and the heart rather than the sum, counts her tiny gift the greatest of all. Facing betrayal and hatred, the disciples are promised that by hypomone, patient endurance, the steadfast holding on of those who belong to Christ, they will gain their souls. When the nations faint at the shaking of the heavens, believers are told to lift up their heads because their apolytrosis, their redemption, the deliverance bought at a price, is drawing near, so that the day of terror for the world is the day of rescue for them. And to be ready they must agrypneo, stay awake and alert, praying for strength to stand before the Son of Man rather than being caught asleep and weighed down by indulgence and worry. Devotion, endurance, hope, and watchfulness mark the people of the coming King.

typology.type: The Son of Man coming with the clouds of heaven, Daniel 7:13-14
typology.fulfillment: The Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and great glory to redeem His people, Luke 21:27.

typology.type: The temple as the dwelling of God with His people
typology.fulfillment: Christ the true temple, whose coming makes the stone temple obsolete, its fall foretold, Luke 21:6.

typology.type: The prophets' days of vengeance on a faithless city
typology.fulfillment: The desolation of Jerusalem, fulfilling all that is written, Luke 21:22.

study.christ: Jesus is the Lord who sees past the clink of large coins to the heart of a poor widow and honors her total gift. He is the true temple, whose earthly counterpart He declares doomed, and whose own body will be destroyed and raised. Above all He is the Son of Man of Daniel's vision, who will come in a cloud with power and great glory, not to terrify His people but to redeem them, so that when the world faints in fear they may lift up their heads. He gives His witnesses words their enemies cannot resist, He preserves them through tribulation so that not a hair perishes eternally, and He speaks words that will outlast heaven and earth. The whole future rests on the reliability of this Christ.
study.god: The God of this chapter looks on the heart and treasures the widow's whole-hearted gift above the surplus of the rich. He is sovereign over the upheavals of history, over the fall of cities and the shaking of the heavens, and He bends even persecution to the spread of the gospel as His people bear witness. He is faithful to preserve His own through the fiercest tribulation and to bring their redemption at the appointed day. And His word is more enduring than the universe itself, so that everything He has promised is certain. This is a God to be trusted with our whole livelihood and our whole future.

crossReferences.note: One like a son of man coming with the clouds, given everlasting dominion, fulfilled in Jesus' promised return.
The parallel Olivet discourse, with the same warnings, signs, and call to watch.
The Macedonians giving out of deep poverty, the whole-hearted generosity the widow embodies.
The grass withers and the flower fades, but the word of our God stands forever, echoed in Luke 21:33.

study.oneStory: In His final public teaching Jesus honors a widow who gave everything and then unveils the age between His comings: the proud temple will fall, His people will suffer and witness among the nations, and the Son of Man will come in glory to redeem. The story stretches from two copper coins in the treasury to the clouds of heaven, and it calls the church of every generation to give without holding back, to endure without fear, to watch without sleeping, and to hope in words that will never pass away.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus say the poor widow gave more than all the rich?

options.text: Because they gave out of their surplus, but she gave all she had to live on.
options.note: Luke 21:4 measures the gift by its cost and completeness, not its size.

options.text: Because God values sacrificial giving from the heart.
options.note: True, but it omits the specific contrast: surplus versus her whole livelihood.

options.text: Because her two coins were secretly worth a great deal.
options.note: The coins were tiny; the point is her total devotion, not hidden value.

options.text: Because the rich gave nothing at all.
options.note: The rich gave large gifts, but from surplus; she gave everything.

quiz.q: What does Jesus foretell about the temple?

options.text: That not one stone will be left on another; it will be completely thrown down.
options.note: Luke 21:6 predicts the temple's total destruction, fulfilled in AD 70.

options.text: That the temple would face coming judgment.
options.note: True, but it omits the striking specificity that not one stone would remain.

options.text: That the temple would be cleansed and restored.
options.note: He foretells its ruin, not its restoration.

options.text: That the temple would stand forever.
options.note: The opposite: He says it will be thrown down.

quiz.q: How are the disciples to face the coming persecution?

options.text: As an opportunity to witness, without worrying beforehand, enduring patiently to gain their souls.
options.note: Luke 21:13-19 turns persecution into witness and calls for patient endurance.

options.text: By enduring the hatred and betrayal that will come.
options.note: True, but it omits that persecution becomes an opportunity for witness with Christ-given words.

options.text: By preparing careful legal defenses in advance.
options.note: Jesus tells them not to worry beforehand; He will give them words to say.

options.text: By avoiding all conflict so they are never arrested.
options.note: He says they will be seized and must witness, not evade at all costs.

quiz.q: How should believers respond when the Son of Man comes with cosmic signs?

options.text: Stand up and lift up their heads, because their redemption is drawing near.
options.note: Luke 21:28 turns the world's terror into the believer's hope of redemption.

options.text: Recognize that the kingdom of God is near.
options.note: True from the fig tree lesson, but it omits the posture of hopeful, head-lifted expectation.

options.text: Faint with fear like the rest of the nations.
options.note: That describes the nations; believers are to lift their heads in hope.

options.text: Hide, because the coming means only judgment for them.
options.note: For His people the coming means redemption, not dread.

quiz.q: What does Jesus command in light of the coming day?

options.text: Keep watch at all times and pray for strength to stand before the Son of Man.
options.note: Luke 21:34-36 calls for constant watchfulness and prayer.

options.text: Be careful not to be weighed down by indulgence and worry.
options.note: True, but it omits the positive command to keep watch and pray to stand.

options.text: Calculate the date so as not to be caught off guard.
options.note: He calls for watchfulness, not date-setting; the day comes suddenly like a snare.

options.text: Assume the day is so far off that readiness can wait.
options.note: The opposite: the day will spring suddenly, so watch always.

questions: The widow gave everything she had to live on. What would it look like for your giving to cost you something real rather than come only from your surplus?
The disciples admired the temple's stones, but Jesus saw its coming ruin. What impressive, seemingly permanent things are you leaning on that will not last?
Jesus turned persecution into an opportunity for witness. When opposition or hostility comes, do you see it as a threat to survive or a moment to testify to Christ?
When the world faints in fear, believers are told to lift up their heads. Does the thought of Christ's return fill you with dread or with hope, and why?
Jesus warns that hearts can be weighed down by indulgence and the worries of life. What is dulling your spiritual watchfulness right now, and what would it mean to keep watch and pray?

translationNotes.note: The widow's gift was two lepta, the smallest coins in circulation, worth a tiny fraction of a day's wage. The smallness of the amount is precisely the point: measured against her poverty, it was everything.

'The times of the Gentiles' marks the era in which Jerusalem is trodden down and the gospel goes out to the nations. It signals a real interval between the fall of the city and the final coming of the Son of Man.

The phrase 'the worries of life' is joined in the printed text to the next clause with an em dash, rendered here as a comma. The warning is that indulgence and anxiety alike can leave a heart unready for the sudden day.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 21:1-38. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text. The em dash in verse 34 was converted to a comma. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
